@graph.name: 9 Materials That Help With Transition Preparation
@graph.description: School has structure, support, and legal protections. Adulthood has none of that automatically. When your child is approaching the transition cliff, they need skills — and you need tools to teach them. Here are 9 materials that help.

mainEntity.name: Why does my daughter turns 18 next year and i'm terrified. she's been in special education since she was three — therapies, ieps, support teams, the whole structure built around her. and then what? she ages out. the services stop. the school that's been her world for 15 years says goodbye. she can't live independently — not yet, maybe not ever in the traditional sense. she can't manage money, can't navigate public transportation alone, can't cook beyond microwaving, can't handle unexpected situations. she doesn't understand that people can take advantage of her. she doesn't know how to advocate for herself at a doctor's office. we've been so focused on academics and behavior that we forgot to prepare her for life. now i'm scrambling. what does she do after high school? is there job training? will anyone hire her? where will she live when we're gone? how do i teach her the thousand skills i do automatically — paying bills, grocery shopping, scheduling appointments, handling emergencies? she has trouble with abstract concepts, so explaining 'budgeting' or 'planning ahead' doesn't work the way it might for other teenagers. she needs to see it, practice it, have it broken into steps she can actually follow. the transition planning meeting at school was a piece of paper with boxes checked. that's not a plan. that's not preparation for a world that doesn't have ieps. i've heard about 'transition services' but no one explains what that actually looks like day to day. i've heard about group homes and supported employment but i don't know how to access them or if they're right for her. i've heard parents say 'we're her plan' but we won't be here forever. who will help her when we're gone? who will make sure she's safe, that she has meaningful work, that she has relationships and community? the school talks about 'post-secondary goals' but those are just words on paper. i need actual materials, actual curricula, actual tools that will teach her the skills she needs for the life she's about to enter. i feel like we've been running toward a cliff for 18 years and only now noticed the edge. how do families actually prepare their children with disabilities for adulthood? what are we supposed to be using?

acceptedAnswer.text: TRANSITION PLANNING / SECONDARY TRANSITION / POST-SECONDARY TRANSITION / LIFE SKILLS DEVELOPMENT / ADAPTIVE BEHAVIOR TRAINING / SELF-DETERMINATION SKILLS! Transition preparation refers to the systematic process of preparing youth with disabilities for the move from school-based services to adult life, including post-secondary education, employment, and independent living. Under IDEA (Individuals with Disabilities Education Act), transition planning must begin by age 16 (or earlier in some states), but meaningful preparation often needs to start much earlier. Transition preparation encompasses multiple domains: EMPLOYMENT/VOCATIONAL (career exploration, job skills, workplace behavior, job-seeking skills, supported employment preparation), EDUCATION/TRAINING (post-secondary options, vocational training, continuing education), INDEPENDENT LIVING (daily living skills, money management, household management, transportation, healthcare, safety), COMMUNITY PARTICIPATION (recreation, relationships, civic engagement, accessing community resources), and SELF-ADVOCACY/SELF-DETERMINATION (understanding one's disability, knowing one's rights, communicating needs, making decisions, setting goals). For families, the transition from the structured, legally-mandated support system of school to the fragmented, eligibility-based adult service system can be bewildering and frightening. The 'cliff' families describe is real — services that were automatic in school must be actively sought, applied for, and often waitlisted in adulthood. Successful transition requires years of preparation, skill-building, and navigation of systems. Materials and curricula designed for transition preparation help structure this process and build the specific skills young people need for adult life.

mainEntity.name: What is Transition Planning / Secondary Transition / Post-Secondary Transition / Life Skills Development / Adaptive Behavior Training / Self-Determination Skills?

acceptedAnswer.text: Transition preparation refers to the systematic process of preparing youth with disabilities for the move from school-based services to adult life, including post-secondary education, employment, and independent living. Under IDEA (Individuals with Disabilities Education Act), transition planning must begin by age 16 (or earlier in some states), but meaningful preparation often needs to start much earlier. Transition preparation encompasses multiple domains: EMPLOYMENT/VOCATIONAL (career exploration, job skills, workplace behavior, job-seeking skills, supported employment preparation), EDUCATION/TRAINING (post-secondary options, vocational training, continuing education), INDEPENDENT LIVING (daily living skills, money management, household management, transportation, healthcare, safety), COMMUNITY PARTICIPATION (recreation, relationships, civic engagement, accessing community resources), and SELF-ADVOCACY/SELF-DETERMINATION (understanding one's disability, knowing one's rights, communicating needs, making decisions, setting goals). For families, the transition from the structured, legally-mandated support system of school to the fragmented, eligibility-based adult service system can be bewildering and frightening. The 'cliff' families describe is real — services that were automatic in school must be actively sought, applied for, and often waitlisted in adulthood. Successful transition requires years of preparation, skill-building, and navigation of systems. Materials and curricula designed for transition preparation help structure this process and build the specific skills young people need for adult life. Common signs include: Approaching transition age (14-22) without clear post-secondary plan, Significant gaps in daily living skills compared to peers, Limited understanding of money, budgeting, or financial concepts, Inability to navigate community independently (transportation, stores, services), Lack of vocational experience or job skills.

mainEntity.name: How does a Transition Assessment Tools and Inventories help with transition planning?

acceptedAnswer.text: Effective transition planning begins with comprehensive assessment — understanding the young person's interests, preferences, strengths, needs, and current skill levels across all transition domains. Transition assessment tools provide structured ways to gather this information systematically. These assessments cover: vocational interests (what types of work appeal to the individual), aptitudes (what they're good at), learning styles, independent living skills (what they can do, what they need to learn), self-determination skills, social skills, community participation, and post-secondary goals. Tools range from formal standardized assessments (like the Transition Planning Inventory or AFLS) to informal interest inventories and skill checklists. The information gathered drives the entire transition plan — you can't plan a route if you don't know the starting point or destination. Many families and schools skip comprehensive assessment and create transition plans based on assumptions or limited information. This leads to goals that don't match the individual's actual interests or abilities. Proper assessment ensures the transition plan is individualized, realistic, and focused on areas that matter most for that specific young person's future. Price: ₹2,000–8,000 (formal tools); many informal tools free. DIY option: Using transition assessments effectively: 1) FORMAL ASSESSMENT TOOLS: Transition Planning Inventory (TPI-2), AFLS (Assessment of Functional Living Skills), ARC Self-Determination Scale, Casey Life Skills, BRIGANCE Transition Skills Inventory — these require purchase but provide comprehensive structured data, 2) INFORMAL ASSESSMENTS: many free checklists and inventories available online; schools often have access to assessment tools, 3) MULTIPLE SOURCES: gather information from the young person (most important), family, teachers, therapists, employers; triangulate perspectives, 4) INTEREST INVENTORIES: critical for vocational planning — what does the person WANT to do? Don't assume based on disability. O*NET Interest Profiler (free online), picture-based interest sorts for those who need visual options, 5) FUNCTIONAL SKILL CHECKLISTS: assess current abilities in daily living, money management, transportation, cooking, self-care, safety — be specific and honest about current levels, 6) SELF-DETERMINATION ASSESSMENT: can the person set goals, make choices, advocate for needs, understand their disability? These skills are highly predictive of adult outcomes, 7) ENVIRONMENTAL ASSESSMENT: what supports exist in the community? What jobs are available? What housing options? What adult services? Assessment should include context, not just the individual, 8) REPEATED ASSESSMENT: transition assessment isn't one-time; reassess annually as skills develop and interests clarify, 9) PERSON-CENTERED: keep the young person at the center — their preferences drive the plan, 10) USE THE DATA: assessment results should directly inform IEP transition goals and post-secondary planning. FAMILY ROLE: families can complete portions of assessments and should review all assessment results. Advocate for comprehensive assessment if school's approach is superficial. Safety: Assessment should identify strengths, not just deficits. Person-centered assessment respects the individual's preferences and dreams. Avoid assessments that only focus on limitations.

mainEntity.name: How does a Life Skills Curricula and Programs help with transition planning?

acceptedAnswer.text: Life skills — the practical abilities needed to function independently in daily life — are often not explicitly taught in traditional academic settings. For youth with disabilities who don't pick up these skills incidentally, structured life skills curricula provide systematic instruction in essential areas: personal care and hygiene, household management (cleaning, laundry, basic maintenance), food preparation and nutrition, money management and budgeting, time management and scheduling, transportation navigation, healthcare management, safety and emergency response, and using technology for daily living. Comprehensive life skills curricula break each skill into teachable components, provide instructional strategies, include practice activities, and offer assessment of progress. Examples include the AFLS (curriculum component), Functional Curriculum for Students with Special Needs, Life Centered Career Education, and various commercial programs. The key is that these curricula treat life skills with the same rigor as academic subjects — with scope and sequence, instructional objectives, and systematic teaching. Many families teach life skills informally at home, which is valuable but may miss gaps or lack the systematic progression that builds competence. Life skills curricula ensure comprehensive coverage and age-appropriate skill development. Price: ₹3,000–15,000 (comprehensive curricula). DIY option: Implementing life skills instruction: 1) COMPREHENSIVE CURRICULA: AFLS Curriculum Guide (matches assessment), Life Centered Career Education (LCCE), Functional Curriculum for Students with Special Needs, various commercial options (Attainment Company, James Stanfield), 2) DOMAIN COVERAGE: ensure curriculum covers ALL domains — personal care, home management, food, money, time, health, safety, transportation, technology, 3) SCOPE AND SEQUENCE: good curricula have logical skill progressions — basic skills before complex, prerequisite skills identified, 4) COMMUNITY-BASED: best life skills instruction happens in real settings, not just classrooms; curricula should support community-based instruction, 5) TASK ANALYSIS: complex skills should be broken into small steps; look for curricula that provide task analyses, 6) VISUAL SUPPORTS: many learners need visual instructions; curricula with pictures, video, or visual task breakdowns are valuable, 7) PRACTICE OPPORTUNITIES: learning requires practice; build in repeated opportunities to practice skills in real contexts, 8) GENERALIZATION: skills learned in one setting should transfer to others; teach in multiple settings and with different materials, 9) ASSESSMENT BUILT-IN: good curricula include ways to track skill acquisition and maintenance, 10) AGE-APPROPRIATE: life skills instruction should use age-appropriate materials and contexts; avoid infantilizing older teens and adults. HOME IMPLEMENTATION: families can use life skills curricula at home to supplement school instruction. Create a home learning environment where the young person practices skills daily. PRIORITIZATION: use assessment results to identify the most critical skills to address first. You can't teach everything at once — prioritize based on the individual's goals and needs. Safety: Life skills instruction should happen in safe, supervised contexts. Kitchen and community safety should be explicitly taught. Supervision level should match current skill level.

mainEntity.name: How does a Visual Task Analysis Cards and Step-by-Step Guides help with transition planning?

acceptedAnswer.text: Many transition-age youth with disabilities learn best when complex tasks are broken into small, sequential steps with visual supports. Visual task analysis cards show each step of a task with photographs or illustrations, allowing the learner to follow along independently. This approach works for everything from daily living tasks (making a bed, doing laundry, preparing a meal) to community skills (using an ATM, ordering at a restaurant, riding the bus) to workplace tasks (clocking in, following a work routine, cleaning procedures). The visual format reduces reliance on verbal memory and instructions, supports independence, and provides a reference the person can use without asking for help. Over time, as skills become automatic, visual supports can be faded. But for many individuals, having visual references available permanently is appropriate and supports ongoing independence. Visual task analysis can be purchased as commercial products (cards, books, apps) or created custom for the individual and their specific environment. Custom visuals using actual photos of the person's workplace, kitchen, or bus route may be most effective for generalization. Price: ₹500–3,000 (commercial); DIY nearly free. DIY option: Creating and using visual task analyses: 1) IDENTIFY THE TASK: choose a specific task the person needs to learn — be very specific (not 'cooking' but 'making a sandwich'), 2) DO THE TASK YOURSELF: actually perform the task, noting every single step; what seems automatic to you may be multiple steps, 3) BREAK INTO STEPS: each step should be one discrete action; if a step is confusing, break it smaller, 4) PHOTOGRAPH EACH STEP: use real photos of the actual environment and materials the person will use; photos are more effective than generic illustrations for many learners, 5) KEEP TEXT MINIMAL: brief labels for each step; rely primarily on the visual, 6) CLEAR SEQUENCE: number steps clearly; use arrows or other sequence indicators, 7) LAMINATE: protect cards for durability and easy cleaning, 8) SIZE APPROPRIATELY: cards should be easy to see and handle in the context where they'll be used; consider a flip ring for portability, 9) PLACE WHERE NEEDED: post visual in the location where task occurs (laundry instructions at washing machine, etc.), 10) TEACH TO USE: explicitly teach the person to follow the visual support; don't assume they'll automatically use it, 11) FADE WHEN READY: as skills become automatic, visual supports can be reduced or removed if the person no longer needs them; but keeping them available is also fine, 12) UPDATE AS NEEDED: if environments or procedures change, update the visuals. APPS AND TECHNOLOGY: apps like Choiceworks, Visual Schedules, and others allow digital task analyses that can include video clips of each step. COMMERCIAL OPTIONS: companies like Attainment, different roads to learning, and others sell pre-made visual task analysis cards for common tasks. Safety: Visual supports for safety-critical tasks (using stove, crossing streets) should include prominent safety reminders. Always assess whether supervision is still needed regardless of visual support availability.

mainEntity.name: How does a Money Management and Financial Literacy Materials help with transition planning?

acceptedAnswer.text: Money management is fundamental to adult independence but is often poorly addressed in transition planning. Youth with disabilities may struggle with abstract concepts like budgeting, saving, and the value of money, making explicit instruction with concrete materials essential. Financial literacy materials for transition include: real and play money for practice, visual budgeting tools (envelopes, charts, picture-based budgets), simplified banking instruction, shopping practice materials, bill-paying guides, wage and paycheck understanding tools, and protection against financial exploitation. The goal is functional money skills — being able to handle the specific money tasks the person will encounter in their life, whether that's paying for lunch, managing a weekly spending allowance, or (for higher-skill individuals) maintaining a bank account and paying bills. For individuals who will need ongoing support with finances, materials focus on understanding money basics, recognizing when they need help, and avoiding exploitation. Financial vulnerability is a significant concern for adults with disabilities, and transition preparation should include explicit teaching about financial safety — not giving out bank information, recognizing scams, understanding that 'friends' who ask for money may be exploiting them. Price: ₹800–4,000. DIY option: Teaching money management for transition: 1) START CONCRETE: use real money; practice identifying coins and bills, making change, counting totals, 2) NEXT-DOLLAR STRATEGY: for those who struggle with exact change, teach 'next dollar' — if item costs ₹47, give ₹50; this functional strategy enables real-world purchasing, 3) PRACTICE PURCHASING: real shopping practice in actual stores; start simple (one item) and increase complexity; teach the full sequence: selecting, paying, receiving change, 4) VISUAL BUDGETS: picture-based budgets show what money goes toward (rent, food, fun); envelope systems make budgeting concrete and tactile, 5) PAYCHECK UNDERSTANDING: teach what a paycheck is, why deductions occur, what 'net pay' means; use real paychecks when possible, 6) BANKING BASICS: if appropriate, teach bank account basics — deposits, withdrawals, checking balances; practice at real ATMs with support, 7) BILL PAYING: for those who will manage bills, teach the concept of recurring expenses; practice with visual calendars showing when bills are due, 8) WANTS vs. NEEDS: explicitly teach the difference; use picture sorts and real-life examples, 9) SAVING: for simple saving, use clear jars where the person can see money accumulating toward a goal, 10) FINANCIAL SAFETY CRITICAL: teach explicitly about scams, people who ask for money, keeping PIN numbers private, what information NOT to share; role-play scenarios; this may be the most important financial skill. SUPPORTED DECISION-MAKING: some individuals will always need support with finances; this doesn't mean they can't participate — teach them to recognize when they need help and who safe helpers are. RESOURCES: Cents and Sensibility curriculum, Practical Money Skills (Visa), Next Step Transition curriculum includes financial components, Money Skills curriculum (Attainment). Safety: Financial exploitation is a serious risk for adults with disabilities. Financial safety instruction is not optional. Supervision and safeguards should continue as long as vulnerability exists. Teach young people to recognize and report financial abuse.

mainEntity.name: How does a Self-Advocacy and Self-Determination Curricula help with transition planning?

acceptedAnswer.text: Self-determination — the ability to make choices, set goals, and direct one's own life — is one of the strongest predictors of positive adult outcomes for people with disabilities. Self-advocacy is a component of self-determination: the ability to speak up for oneself, explain one's needs, and request appropriate supports and accommodations. Throughout childhood, others (parents, teachers, therapists) advocate FOR the child. In adulthood, the individual must increasingly advocate for themselves — in employment, in healthcare, in services, in daily life. Self-determination and self-advocacy curricula explicitly teach: understanding one's own disability (what it is, how it affects them, what supports help), knowing one's rights (ADA, service entitlements, workplace accommodations), setting personal goals, making decisions, communicating needs effectively, participating in planning meetings, and connecting with disability community and resources. Materials range from comprehensive curricula (TAKE CHARGE, Whose Future Is It Anyway?, Self-Advocacy Strategy) to specific tools for IEP participation, disclosure decisions, and accommodation requests. For many young people with disabilities, these concepts need to be taught explicitly and practiced extensively before they can be used independently. Price: ₹2,000–6,000. DIY option: Building self-advocacy and self-determination: 1) UNDERSTANDING DISABILITY: help the young person understand their own disability — what it is, how it affects them, what's hard and what's not related to disability; use age-appropriate language; this is foundation for self-advocacy, 2) STRENGTHS AND NEEDS: can they articulate what they're good at AND what they need help with? Practice stating both, 3) ACCOMMODATION KNOWLEDGE: do they know what accommodations help them? Can they request those accommodations? Practice the language: 'I learn better when...', 'I need...', 'Could I have...', 4) RIGHTS AWARENESS: teach basic disability rights — right to accommodations at work, right to services, right to be treated with respect; doesn't need to be legal expertise, but basic awareness, 5) IEP/ISP PARTICIPATION: young person should attend and participate in their planning meetings; start with small roles and increase, 6) CHOICE-MAKING: provide daily opportunities for meaningful choices; build decision-making skills in low-stakes contexts before high-stakes, 7) GOAL-SETTING: help them set personal goals (not just school/therapy goals); practice the process of identifying what they want and steps to get there, 8) SPEAKING UP PRACTICE: role-play advocacy scenarios — asking for help, requesting accommodation, disagreeing respectfully, reporting problems, 9) CONNECTING TO COMMUNITY: connect with disability advocacy organizations, self-advocate groups, role models with similar disabilities; they're not alone, 10) CURRICULA: TAKE CHARGE for Youth, Whose Future Is It Anyway? (Zarrow Center), Self-Advocacy Strategy, I'm Determined project, NEXT S.T.E.P. curriculum. FAMILY ROLE: gradually transfer advocacy responsibilities to the young person; resist doing for them what they can do for themselves; model self-advocacy in your own life. Safety: Self-advocacy should be balanced with safety awareness. Young people should also learn when NOT to disclose information and how to recognize when someone is taking advantage of them.

mainEntity.name: How does a Vocational Skills Training Materials help with transition planning?

acceptedAnswer.text: Employment is central to adult identity, financial security, and community integration. Vocational skills training materials prepare transition-age youth for work by teaching both specific job skills and general workplace competencies. Materials in this category include: job-specific skill training (task analyses for particular jobs, skill simulations), workplace behavior curricula (punctuality, following directions, interacting with supervisors and coworkers, handling criticism, workplace safety), job-seeking skills (applications, resumes, interviews, disclosure decisions), and career exploration tools (interest inventories, job sampling guides, informational interview protocols). Effective vocational preparation also happens through work-based learning experiences — internships, job shadowing, volunteer work, paid employment — which should be supported by these instructional materials. For youth who will enter supported employment, materials help develop the skills that will be supplemented by job coaching. For those heading toward competitive employment, materials address the full range of job-seeking and job-keeping skills. Vocational preparation should begin early (career exploration by age 14, work experiences by age 16) and should be directly connected to the young person's interests and abilities identified in transition assessment. Price: ₹2,500–10,000. DIY option: Vocational preparation implementation: 1) CAREER EXPLORATION: before training for specific jobs, explore interests and options; use interest inventories, job shadowing, informational interviews, videos about different careers, 2) WORK-BASED LEARNING: the most important vocational preparation is REAL work experience; push for job shadowing, internships, volunteer positions, and paid employment starting in high school, 3) WORKPLACE BEHAVIOR CURRICULA: teach the 'soft skills' that determine job success — punctuality, attendance, following directions, accepting feedback, interacting appropriately with coworkers/supervisors, workplace safety, asking for help, 4) JOB-SPECIFIC SKILLS: once a career direction is identified, train the specific skills needed; use task analyses, simulations, and real-world practice, 5) JOB-SEEKING SKILLS: teach how to find job openings, complete applications, create a resume (if appropriate), practice interviewing, discuss disclosure decisions (when and how to tell employers about disability), 6) PRACTICE INTERVIEWS: repeated practice of job interviews reduces anxiety and improves performance; use video self-review if possible, 7) WORKPLACE ACCOMMODATIONS: teach the young person what accommodations might help them at work and how to request them, 8) PROBLEM-SOLVING: workplace problems will occur; teach how to handle common challenges (criticism, conflict with coworker, confusion about tasks), 9) UNDERSTAND EMPLOYMENT: what is a paycheck? What are taxes? What does 'full-time' vs. 'part-time' mean? What are benefits? Build understanding of employment concepts, 10) SUPPORTED EMPLOYMENT PREP: for those who will need job coaching, prepare them to work effectively with a job coach. RESOURCES: Job-Specific Task Analyses, Workplace Social Skills curriculum, Transition Curriculum from various publishers, Project SEARCH materials, job sampling protocols. Safety: Workplace safety must be explicitly taught. Young people should know their rights in the workplace and how to report unsafe conditions or treatment. Watch for exploitation — unpaid or underpaid work is not acceptable.

mainEntity.name: What are the warning signs that need professional help?

acceptedAnswer.text: REAL WORLD EVIDENCE from Pinnacle Blooms Network: 20M+ Exclusive 1:1 Sessions. 97%+ Measured Improvement. 70+ Centers.

@graph.name: How to Help a Child with Transition Planning
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. Transition from school to adulthood is one of the most significant life changes for youth with disabilities and their families. Unlike the entitlement-based system of school (where eligible students automatically receive services), the adult service system is eligibility-based with limited capacity — not everyone who needs services receives them. This 'transition cliff' requires years of preparation: building the functional skills for adult life (daily living, employment, community navigation, self-advocacy) AND navigating the systems that provide adult supports (vocational rehabilitation, developmental disability services, Social Security, housing). The most robust predictor of positive adult outcomes is self-determination — the individual's ability to make choices, set goals, understand themselves, and advocate for their needs. Transition preparation materials provide the structure for this complex, multi-year process of skill-building and system navigation. Transition planning should be person-centered, starting with the young person's interests, preferences, and goals — not just available program slots. The goal is a meaningful adult life with appropriate supports, not just program placement.

supply.name: Visual Task Analysis Cards and Step-by-Step Guides

step.name: Understand the Problem
step.text: Transition preparation refers to the systematic process of preparing youth with disabilities for the move from school-based services to adult life, including post-secondary education, employment, and independent living. Under IDEA (Individuals with Disabilities Education Act), transition planning must begin by age 16 (or earlier in some states), but meaningful preparation often needs to start much earlier. Transition preparation encompasses multiple domains: EMPLOYMENT/VOCATIONAL (career exploration, job skills, workplace behavior, job-seeking skills, supported employment preparation), EDUCATION/TRAINING (post-secondary options, vocational training, continuing education), INDEPENDENT LIVING (daily living skills, money management, household management, transportation, healthcare, safety), COMMUNITY PARTICIPATION (recreation, relationships, civic engagement, accessing community resources), and SELF-ADVOCACY/SELF-DETERMINATION (understanding one's disability, knowing one's rights, communicating needs, making decisions, setting goals). For families, the transition from the structured, legally-mandated support system of school to the fragmented, eligibility-based adult service system can be bewildering and frightening. The 'cliff' families describe is real — services that were automatic in school must be actively sought, applied for, and often waitlisted in adulthood. Successful transition requires years of preparation, skill-building, and navigation of systems. Materials and curricula designed for transition preparation help structure this process and build the specific skills young people need for adult life. Transition from school to adulthood is one of the most significant life changes for youth with disabilities and their families. Unlike the entitlement-based system of school (where eligible students automatically receive services), the adult service system is eligibility-based with limited capacity — not everyone who needs services receives them. This 'transition cliff' requires years of preparation: building the functional skills for adult life (daily living, employment, community navigation, self-advocacy) AND navigating the systems that provide adult supports (vocational rehabilitation, developmental disability services, Social Security, housing). The most robust predictor of positive adult outcomes is self-determination — the individual's ability to make choices, set goals, understand themselves, and advocate for their needs. Transition preparation materials provide the structure for this complex, multi-year process of skill-building and system navigation. Transition planning should be person-centered, starting with the young person's interests, preferences, and goals — not just available program slots. The goal is a meaningful adult life with appropriate supports, not just program placement.

step.name: Start with Foundation Materials
step.text: Begin with: Transition Assessment Tools and Inventories, Life Skills Curricula and Programs, Visual Task Analysis Cards and Step-by-Step Guides.

signOrSymptom.name: Approaching transition age (14-22) without clear post-secondary plan
Significant gaps in daily living skills compared to peers
Limited understanding of money, budgeting, or financial concepts
Inability to navigate community independently (transportation, stores, services)
Lack of vocational experience or job skills
Difficulty with self-advocacy — cannot explain needs or request accommodations
Limited understanding of own disability and its implications

possibleTreatment.name: Sensory Integration Therapy
possibleTreatment.description: Occupational therapy using graded sensory input

@graph.name: 9 Materials for Transition Planning
@graph.description: Evidence-based materials for ages 14–22+ years (transition age, with preparation beginning earlier for foundational skills)

item.name: Transition Assessment Tools and Inventories
item.description: Effective transition planning begins with comprehensive assessment — understanding the young person's interests, preferences, strengths, needs, and current skill levels across all transition domains. Transition assessment tools provide structured ways to gather this information systematically. These assessments cover: vocational interests (what types of work appeal to the individual), aptitudes (what they're good at), learning styles, independent living skills (what they can do, what they need to learn), self-determination skills, social skills, community participation, and post-secondary goals. Tools range from formal standardized assessments (like the Transition Planning Inventory or AFLS) to informal interest inventories and skill checklists. The information gathered drives the entire transition plan — you can't plan a route if you don't know the starting point or destination. Many families and schools skip comprehensive assessment and create transition plans based on assumptions or limited information. This leads to goals that don't match the individual's actual interests or abilities. Proper assessment ensures the transition plan is individualized, realistic, and focused on areas that matter most for that specific young person's future.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Using transition assessments effectively: 1) FORMAL ASSESSMENT TOOLS: Transition Planning Inventory (TPI-2), AFLS (Assessment of Functional Living Skills), ARC Self-Determination Scale, Casey Life Skills, BRIGANCE Transition Skills Inventory — these require purchase but provide comprehensive structured data, 2) INFORMAL ASSESSMENTS: many free checklists and inventories available online; schools often have access to assessment tools, 3) MULTIPLE SOURCES: gather information from the young person (most important), family, teachers, therapists, employers; triangulate perspectives, 4) INTEREST INVENTORIES: critical for vocational planning — what does the person WANT to do? Don't assume based on disability. O*NET Interest Profiler (free online), picture-based interest sorts for those who need visual options, 5) FUNCTIONAL SKILL CHECKLISTS: assess current abilities in daily living, money management, transportation, cooking, self-care, safety — be specific and honest about current levels, 6) SELF-DETERMINATION ASSESSMENT: can the person set goals, make choices, advocate for needs, understand their disability? These skills are highly predictive of adult outcomes, 7) ENVIRONMENTAL ASSESSMENT: what supports exist in the community? What jobs are available? What housing options? What adult services? Assessment should include context, not just the individual, 8) REPEATED ASSESSMENT: transition assessment isn't one-time; reassess annually as skills develop and interests clarify, 9) PERSON-CENTERED: keep the young person at the center — their preferences drive the plan, 10) USE THE DATA: assessment results should directly inform IEP transition goals and post-secondary planning. FAMILY ROLE: families can complete portions of assessments and should review all assessment results. Advocate for comprehensive assessment if school's approach is superficial.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Assessment should identify strengths, not just deficits. Person-centered assessment respects the individual's preferences and dreams. Avoid assessments that only focus on limitations.

item.name: Life Skills Curricula and Programs
item.description: Life skills — the practical abilities needed to function independently in daily life — are often not explicitly taught in traditional academic settings. For youth with disabilities who don't pick up these skills incidentally, structured life skills curricula provide systematic instruction in essential areas: personal care and hygiene, household management (cleaning, laundry, basic maintenance), food preparation and nutrition, money management and budgeting, time management and scheduling, transportation navigation, healthcare management, safety and emergency response, and using technology for daily living. Comprehensive life skills curricula break each skill into teachable components, provide instructional strategies, include practice activities, and offer assessment of progress. Examples include the AFLS (curriculum component), Functional Curriculum for Students with Special Needs, Life Centered Career Education, and various commercial programs. The key is that these curricula treat life skills with the same rigor as academic subjects — with scope and sequence, instructional objectives, and systematic teaching. Many families teach life skills informally at home, which is valuable but may miss gaps or lack the systematic progression that builds competence. Life skills curricula ensure comprehensive coverage and age-appropriate skill development.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Implementing life skills instruction: 1) COMPREHENSIVE CURRICULA: AFLS Curriculum Guide (matches assessment), Life Centered Career Education (LCCE), Functional Curriculum for Students with Special Needs, various commercial options (Attainment Company, James Stanfield), 2) DOMAIN COVERAGE: ensure curriculum covers ALL domains — personal care, home management, food, money, time, health, safety, transportation, technology, 3) SCOPE AND SEQUENCE: good curricula have logical skill progressions — basic skills before complex, prerequisite skills identified, 4) COMMUNITY-BASED: best life skills instruction happens in real settings, not just classrooms; curricula should support community-based instruction, 5) TASK ANALYSIS: complex skills should be broken into small steps; look for curricula that provide task analyses, 6) VISUAL SUPPORTS: many learners need visual instructions; curricula with pictures, video, or visual task breakdowns are valuable, 7) PRACTICE OPPORTUNITIES: learning requires practice; build in repeated opportunities to practice skills in real contexts, 8) GENERALIZATION: skills learned in one setting should transfer to others; teach in multiple settings and with different materials, 9) ASSESSMENT BUILT-IN: good curricula include ways to track skill acquisition and maintenance, 10) AGE-APPROPRIATE: life skills instruction should use age-appropriate materials and contexts; avoid infantilizing older teens and adults. HOME IMPLEMENTATION: families can use life skills curricula at home to supplement school instruction. Create a home learning environment where the young person practices skills daily. PRIORITIZATION: use assessment results to identify the most critical skills to address first. You can't teach everything at once — prioritize based on the individual's goals and needs.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Life skills instruction should happen in safe, supervised contexts. Kitchen and community safety should be explicitly taught. Supervision level should match current skill level.

item.name: Visual Task Analysis Cards and Step-by-Step Guides
item.description: Many transition-age youth with disabilities learn best when complex tasks are broken into small, sequential steps with visual supports. Visual task analysis cards show each step of a task with photographs or illustrations, allowing the learner to follow along independently. This approach works for everything from daily living tasks (making a bed, doing laundry, preparing a meal) to community skills (using an ATM, ordering at a restaurant, riding the bus) to workplace tasks (clocking in, following a work routine, cleaning procedures). The visual format reduces reliance on verbal memory and instructions, supports independence, and provides a reference the person can use without asking for help. Over time, as skills become automatic, visual supports can be faded. But for many individuals, having visual references available permanently is appropriate and supports ongoing independence. Visual task analysis can be purchased as commercial products (cards, books, apps) or created custom for the individual and their specific environment. Custom visuals using actual photos of the person's workplace, kitchen, or bus route may be most effective for generalization.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Creating and using visual task analyses: 1) IDENTIFY THE TASK: choose a specific task the person needs to learn — be very specific (not 'cooking' but 'making a sandwich'), 2) DO THE TASK YOURSELF: actually perform the task, noting every single step; what seems automatic to you may be multiple steps, 3) BREAK INTO STEPS: each step should be one discrete action; if a step is confusing, break it smaller, 4) PHOTOGRAPH EACH STEP: use real photos of the actual environment and materials the person will use; photos are more effective than generic illustrations for many learners, 5) KEEP TEXT MINIMAL: brief labels for each step; rely primarily on the visual, 6) CLEAR SEQUENCE: number steps clearly; use arrows or other sequence indicators, 7) LAMINATE: protect cards for durability and easy cleaning, 8) SIZE APPROPRIATELY: cards should be easy to see and handle in the context where they'll be used; consider a flip ring for portability, 9) PLACE WHERE NEEDED: post visual in the location where task occurs (laundry instructions at washing machine, etc.), 10) TEACH TO USE: explicitly teach the person to follow the visual support; don't assume they'll automatically use it, 11) FADE WHEN READY: as skills become automatic, visual supports can be reduced or removed if the person no longer needs them; but keeping them available is also fine, 12) UPDATE AS NEEDED: if environments or procedures change, update the visuals. APPS AND TECHNOLOGY: apps like Choiceworks, Visual Schedules, and others allow digital task analyses that can include video clips of each step. COMMERCIAL OPTIONS: companies like Attainment, different roads to learning, and others sell pre-made visual task analysis cards for common tasks.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Visual supports for safety-critical tasks (using stove, crossing streets) should include prominent safety reminders. Always assess whether supervision is still needed regardless of visual support availability.

item.name: Money Management and Financial Literacy Materials
item.description: Money management is fundamental to adult independence but is often poorly addressed in transition planning. Youth with disabilities may struggle with abstract concepts like budgeting, saving, and the value of money, making explicit instruction with concrete materials essential. Financial literacy materials for transition include: real and play money for practice, visual budgeting tools (envelopes, charts, picture-based budgets), simplified banking instruction, shopping practice materials, bill-paying guides, wage and paycheck understanding tools, and protection against financial exploitation. The goal is functional money skills — being able to handle the specific money tasks the person will encounter in their life, whether that's paying for lunch, managing a weekly spending allowance, or (for higher-skill individuals) maintaining a bank account and paying bills. For individuals who will need ongoing support with finances, materials focus on understanding money basics, recognizing when they need help, and avoiding exploitation. Financial vulnerability is a significant concern for adults with disabilities, and transition preparation should include explicit teaching about financial safety — not giving out bank information, recognizing scams, understanding that 'friends' who ask for money may be exploiting them.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Teaching money management for transition: 1) START CONCRETE: use real money; practice identifying coins and bills, making change, counting totals, 2) NEXT-DOLLAR STRATEGY: for those who struggle with exact change, teach 'next dollar' — if item costs ₹47, give ₹50; this functional strategy enables real-world purchasing, 3) PRACTICE PURCHASING: real shopping practice in actual stores; start simple (one item) and increase complexity; teach the full sequence: selecting, paying, receiving change, 4) VISUAL BUDGETS: picture-based budgets show what money goes toward (rent, food, fun); envelope systems make budgeting concrete and tactile, 5) PAYCHECK UNDERSTANDING: teach what a paycheck is, why deductions occur, what 'net pay' means; use real paychecks when possible, 6) BANKING BASICS: if appropriate, teach bank account basics — deposits, withdrawals, checking balances; practice at real ATMs with support, 7) BILL PAYING: for those who will manage bills, teach the concept of recurring expenses; practice with visual calendars showing when bills are due, 8) WANTS vs. NEEDS: explicitly teach the difference; use picture sorts and real-life examples, 9) SAVING: for simple saving, use clear jars where the person can see money accumulating toward a goal, 10) FINANCIAL SAFETY CRITICAL: teach explicitly about scams, people who ask for money, keeping PIN numbers private, what information NOT to share; role-play scenarios; this may be the most important financial skill. SUPPORTED DECISION-MAKING: some individuals will always need support with finances; this doesn't mean they can't participate — teach them to recognize when they need help and who safe helpers are. RESOURCES: Cents and Sensibility curriculum, Practical Money Skills (Visa), Next Step Transition curriculum includes financial components, Money Skills curriculum (Attainment).

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Financial exploitation is a serious risk for adults with disabilities. Financial safety instruction is not optional. Supervision and safeguards should continue as long as vulnerability exists. Teach young people to recognize and report financial abuse.

item.name: Self-Advocacy and Self-Determination Curricula
item.description: Self-determination — the ability to make choices, set goals, and direct one's own life — is one of the strongest predictors of positive adult outcomes for people with disabilities. Self-advocacy is a component of self-determination: the ability to speak up for oneself, explain one's needs, and request appropriate supports and accommodations. Throughout childhood, others (parents, teachers, therapists) advocate FOR the child. In adulthood, the individual must increasingly advocate for themselves — in employment, in healthcare, in services, in daily life. Self-determination and self-advocacy curricula explicitly teach: understanding one's own disability (what it is, how it affects them, what supports help), knowing one's rights (ADA, service entitlements, workplace accommodations), setting personal goals, making decisions, communicating needs effectively, participating in planning meetings, and connecting with disability community and resources. Materials range from comprehensive curricula (TAKE CHARGE, Whose Future Is It Anyway?, Self-Advocacy Strategy) to specific tools for IEP participation, disclosure decisions, and accommodation requests. For many young people with disabilities, these concepts need to be taught explicitly and practiced extensively before they can be used independently.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Building self-advocacy and self-determination: 1) UNDERSTANDING DISABILITY: help the young person understand their own disability — what it is, how it affects them, what's hard and what's not related to disability; use age-appropriate language; this is foundation for self-advocacy, 2) STRENGTHS AND NEEDS: can they articulate what they're good at AND what they need help with? Practice stating both, 3) ACCOMMODATION KNOWLEDGE: do they know what accommodations help them? Can they request those accommodations? Practice the language: 'I learn better when...', 'I need...', 'Could I have...', 4) RIGHTS AWARENESS: teach basic disability rights — right to accommodations at work, right to services, right to be treated with respect; doesn't need to be legal expertise, but basic awareness, 5) IEP/ISP PARTICIPATION: young person should attend and participate in their planning meetings; start with small roles and increase, 6) CHOICE-MAKING: provide daily opportunities for meaningful choices; build decision-making skills in low-stakes contexts before high-stakes, 7) GOAL-SETTING: help them set personal goals (not just school/therapy goals); practice the process of identifying what they want and steps to get there, 8) SPEAKING UP PRACTICE: role-play advocacy scenarios — asking for help, requesting accommodation, disagreeing respectfully, reporting problems, 9) CONNECTING TO COMMUNITY: connect with disability advocacy organizations, self-advocate groups, role models with similar disabilities; they're not alone, 10) CURRICULA: TAKE CHARGE for Youth, Whose Future Is It Anyway? (Zarrow Center), Self-Advocacy Strategy, I'm Determined project, NEXT S.T.E.P. curriculum. FAMILY ROLE: gradually transfer advocacy responsibilities to the young person; resist doing for them what they can do for themselves; model self-advocacy in your own life.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Self-advocacy should be balanced with safety awareness. Young people should also learn when NOT to disclose information and how to recognize when someone is taking advantage of them.

item.name: Vocational Skills Training Materials
item.description: Employment is central to adult identity, financial security, and community integration. Vocational skills training materials prepare transition-age youth for work by teaching both specific job skills and general workplace competencies. Materials in this category include: job-specific skill training (task analyses for particular jobs, skill simulations), workplace behavior curricula (punctuality, following directions, interacting with supervisors and coworkers, handling criticism, workplace safety), job-seeking skills (applications, resumes, interviews, disclosure decisions), and career exploration tools (interest inventories, job sampling guides, informational interview protocols). Effective vocational preparation also happens through work-based learning experiences — internships, job shadowing, volunteer work, paid employment — which should be supported by these instructional materials. For youth who will enter supported employment, materials help develop the skills that will be supplemented by job coaching. For those heading toward competitive employment, materials address the full range of job-seeking and job-keeping skills. Vocational preparation should begin early (career exploration by age 14, work experiences by age 16) and should be directly connected to the young person's interests and abilities identified in transition assessment.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Vocational preparation implementation: 1) CAREER EXPLORATION: before training for specific jobs, explore interests and options; use interest inventories, job shadowing, informational interviews, videos about different careers, 2) WORK-BASED LEARNING: the most important vocational preparation is REAL work experience; push for job shadowing, internships, volunteer positions, and paid employment starting in high school, 3) WORKPLACE BEHAVIOR CURRICULA: teach the 'soft skills' that determine job success — punctuality, attendance, following directions, accepting feedback, interacting appropriately with coworkers/supervisors, workplace safety, asking for help, 4) JOB-SPECIFIC SKILLS: once a career direction is identified, train the specific skills needed; use task analyses, simulations, and real-world practice, 5) JOB-SEEKING SKILLS: teach how to find job openings, complete applications, create a resume (if appropriate), practice interviewing, discuss disclosure decisions (when and how to tell employers about disability), 6) PRACTICE INTERVIEWS: repeated practice of job interviews reduces anxiety and improves performance; use video self-review if possible, 7) WORKPLACE ACCOMMODATIONS: teach the young person what accommodations might help them at work and how to request them, 8) PROBLEM-SOLVING: workplace problems will occur; teach how to handle common challenges (criticism, conflict with coworker, confusion about tasks), 9) UNDERSTAND EMPLOYMENT: what is a paycheck? What are taxes? What does 'full-time' vs. 'part-time' mean? What are benefits? Build understanding of employment concepts, 10) SUPPORTED EMPLOYMENT PREP: for those who will need job coaching, prepare them to work effectively with a job coach. RESOURCES: Job-Specific Task Analyses, Workplace Social Skills curriculum, Transition Curriculum from various publishers, Project SEARCH materials, job sampling protocols.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Workplace safety must be explicitly taught. Young people should know their rights in the workplace and how to report unsafe conditions or treatment. Watch for exploitation — unpaid or underpaid work is not acceptable.

item.name: Community Navigation and Safety Materials
item.description: Adult independence requires safe, effective navigation of the community — using public transportation, accessing stores and services, responding to emergencies, and staying safe from exploitation. Community navigation materials teach these skills through visual supports, social narratives, video modeling, and practice activities. Transportation training materials include: visual guides to bus/metro routes, practice activities for purchasing tickets, scripts for asking directions, safety rules for public transportation, and independence-building progressions (from fully supported to independent travel). Safety materials address: recognizing dangerous situations, responding to emergencies (calling for help, basic first aid), stranger safety, exploitation awareness (recognizing when someone is taking advantage), and personal safety practices. For many families, safety fears are the biggest barrier to community independence. Structured safety training, combined with graduated practice and appropriate supervision levels, builds competence while managing risk. Community-based instruction — practicing skills in real community settings, not just classrooms — is the most effective approach, supported by instructional materials.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Building community navigation and safety: 1) COMMUNITY-BASED INSTRUCTION: the most important element — actual practice in real community settings, not just classroom simulations; go to stores, ride buses, visit services, 2) TRANSPORTATION TRAINING: identify the transportation system the person will use; create visual guides to specific routes they need; practice the full sequence repeatedly; gradually increase independence, 3) TRAVEL TRAINING PROGRESSION: fully accompanied → person leads while accompanied → spot-checks (adult follows at distance) → independent with check-ins → full independence; match level to competence, 4) SAFETY SCRIPTS: practice what to say if lost, if approached by strangers, if there's an emergency; drill until automatic, 5) EMERGENCY RESPONSE: teach how to call for help (phone, emergency numbers), what information to give, when to seek help; practice with role-play, 6) STRANGER SAFETY: explicitly teach which people are safe to approach for help (employees, police, others in uniform) and how to recognize concerning behavior, 7) EXPLOITATION AWARENESS: teach about people who might try to take advantage — asking for money, 'friendship' that demands money/favors, requests for personal information; practice saying no and getting away, 8) PEDESTRIAN SAFETY: crossing streets, navigating parking lots, walking in unfamiliar areas safely, 9) PHONE SKILLS: can they use a phone to call for help, to navigate (GPS), to contact family? Practice actual phone use, 10) GRADUAL EXPANSION: start with familiar, close-by community locations; gradually expand range and complexity. RESOURCES: Travel training curricula, community safety programs, social narratives about community situations, video modeling for transportation. APPS: GPS and transit apps can support independence for those who can use technology.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Community safety must be addressed before independence expands. Supervision levels should match current skills. Some individuals will need ongoing support for safe community access. Build skills but maintain appropriate safeguards.

item.name: Healthcare Self-Management Materials
item.description: Managing one's own health becomes increasingly important in adulthood, but many transition-age youth with disabilities have had all healthcare managed by parents. Healthcare self-management materials teach: understanding one's own health conditions and medications, communicating with healthcare providers, scheduling and keeping appointments, recognizing when to seek medical help, maintaining healthy habits, and navigating the healthcare system. For young people with chronic health conditions, mental health needs, or ongoing medication, this transition is particularly critical. Materials include medical ID cards the person carries, health information summaries they can share with providers, visual supports for medication management, social stories about medical appointments, and curricula for health decision-making. Self-advocacy skills are essential in healthcare settings — being able to describe symptoms, answer questions, ask for clarification, and consent to or refuse treatment. For individuals who will need supported healthcare management, materials focus on participating to the maximum extent possible while recognizing when to involve support people. The goal is maximum appropriate autonomy in healthcare, with safety and support as needed.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Teaching healthcare self-management: 1) HEALTH HISTORY KNOWLEDGE: does the young person know their diagnoses, allergies, and medications? Create a health summary card they carry and can share with providers, 2) MEDICATION MANAGEMENT: teach what medications they take, why, when, and how; use visual medication schedules and pill organizers; practice the routine, 3) APPOINTMENT PREPARATION: prepare for appointments — what questions to ask, what information to bring, what the appointment is for; use checklists, 4) COMMUNICATION WITH PROVIDERS: practice describing symptoms, answering questions, asking for clarification, 5) CONSENT AND REFUSAL: teach that they have the right to consent to or refuse treatment (with appropriate support for complex decisions); practice saying 'I don't understand' or 'I want to think about it', 6) RECOGNIZING HEALTH NEEDS: when should they seek medical care? Teach basic symptom recognition and when to get help, 7) HEALTHY HABITS: nutrition, exercise, sleep, hygiene — teach the habits that support health, 8) MENTAL HEALTH AWARENESS: teach about their own mental health needs if applicable; how to recognize when they need help; how to access mental health support, 9) TRANSITION OF CARE: work with pediatric providers to plan the transition to adult healthcare; prepare the young person for the differences, 10) NAVIGATING THE SYSTEM: how do appointments get scheduled? How does insurance work (basics)? Where is the pharmacy? Practice the logistics. RESOURCES: Healthy Transitions curricula, Got Transition resources, healthcare self-advocacy tools, medication management systems. IMPORTANT: For individuals with significant healthcare needs, the family and healthcare team should collaborate on what level of self-management is appropriate and what supports are needed.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Healthcare self-management should match cognitive ability and the complexity of health needs. Some individuals will need ongoing support for medication management, appointments, and health decisions. Safety comes before independence in healthcare.

item.name: Transition Planning Guides and Navigation Tools
item.description: Transition involves not just skill-building but also navigation of complex systems — school transition services, adult disability services, vocational rehabilitation, Social Security benefits, housing options, legal considerations (guardianship, alternatives to guardianship), and more. Transition planning guides help families and young people understand and navigate these systems. Tools in this category include: transition planning timelines (what to do when), checklists and organizers for the transition process, guides to adult service systems (how to apply, what to expect), benefits planning information, legal/financial planning guides (special needs trusts, ABLE accounts, guardianship alternatives), and community resource directories. Many families approach transition uninformed about the systems they're entering, leading to missed deadlines, failed applications, and gaps in services. Transition planning guides provide the roadmap — what steps to take, when to take them, and how to access available resources. While professionals (transition coordinators, social workers) should provide guidance, families benefit from having their own resources to understand the process and advocate effectively. Knowledge is power in navigating bureaucratic systems.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Navigating the transition planning process: 1) TRANSITION TIMELINE: create or obtain a timeline showing key transition milestones — when assessment starts, when planning meetings occur, when applications are due, when services transfer, 2) ANNUAL PLANNING: transition goals should be reviewed and updated at least annually in IEP; be proactive, not passive, in these meetings, 3) UNDERSTAND ADULT SYSTEMS: learn what adult services exist — vocational rehabilitation, developmental disability services, mental health services, Social Security benefits — and their eligibility criteria, 4) APPLICATION TIMELINES: many adult services have long waitlists; apply early (often years before aging out); know the deadlines, 5) VOCATIONAL REHABILITATION: connect with VR services while still in school; they can provide job training, job placement, and ongoing support, 6) BENEFITS PLANNING: understand how work affects benefits (SSI, Medicaid); consult a benefits counselor if the young person receives benefits, 7) LEGAL PLANNING: understand guardianship alternatives (power of attorney, supported decision-making, representative payee); don't assume full guardianship is necessary, 8) FINANCIAL PLANNING: learn about special needs trusts, ABLE accounts, and other tools that protect benefits while building resources, 9) HOUSING EXPLORATION: explore housing options early — family home, supported living, group homes, independent living; understand what's available and how to access it, 10) CREATE A TRANSITION BINDER: keep all transition documents, plans, and information organized and accessible; bring it to meetings. RESOURCES: Wrightslaw transition resources, PACER Center transition guides, state-specific transition guides, Think College (for post-secondary education), APSE (for employment), The Arc (for full-life advocacy). LOCAL RESOURCES: connect with your local Parent Training and Information Center (PTI), developmental disability agency, and disability advocacy organizations.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Transition planning information varies by location; always verify with local agencies. Be cautious of for-profit services that promise results for large fees — many resources are available free through public agencies and nonprofit organizations.

socialMedia.caption: When school ends and the real world begins — and you realize the safety net is about to disappear. The IEP ends. The therapies stop. The structure that's been your child's world for 15+ years says goodbye. What comes next? Here are 9 materials that help with transition preparation: Transition assessments, life skills curricula, visual task analysis, money management materials, self-advocacy curricula, vocational training, community navigation tools, healthcare self-management, and transition planning guides. From aging out to growing into. From protected student to capable adult. From 'what do we do now' to 'here's the plan.' 💜 #TransitionPlanning #AdultWithDisability #LifeSkills #PinnacleBlooms
